prompt: A meticulously engraved scientific illustration in the style of 19th-century naturalist engravings (1800–1900), resembling the work of explorers and natural historians. The image depicts a scholarly naturalist or surveyor descending a complex mountainside, carefully measuring and following the steepest gradient at each step. The landscape is rendered in precise cross-hatched detail with clear contour lines showing the mountain's topology. The surveyor uses measuring instruments (theodolite, surveying staff) and wears period-appropriate clothing. The technique employs hand-engraved cross-hatching for all shading and texture—no photographic realism. Each contour and ridge is clearly visible, mapping the gradient field. The palette is muted: sepia, ochre, grey, charcoal—typical of engraved plates. The composition uses linear perspective and careful spatial organization. A deeper valley is visible in the far distance, unseen by the descending surveyor—illustrating how the algorithm follows local information, not global knowledge. The border and margins are clean and formal, like a specimen study from a naturalist journal. No modern elements, no text within the image, no legend. Completely hand-engraved aesthetic, not watercolour or painting. Museum-quality scientific documentation from the Age of Exploration.
tradition_approach: Hand-engraved cross-hatching for shading and texture; linear perspective compositional structure; muted earth-tone palette (ochre, sepia, grey); specimen-study framing with careful attention to anatomical or topographical detail; margins and bordered frame.
